coredata.dc:title: A comparison of batch and semi-batch reactors for leaching battery cathodes (LiCoO2) under controlled addition of HCl and H2O2
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Cerrillo-Gonzalez, Maria del Mar
Villen-Guzman, Maria
Rivas-Bascon, Alvaro
Rodriguez-Maroto, Jose Miguel
Paz-Garcia, Juan Manuel
coredata.dc:description: Recovery of cobalt and lithium from end-of-life Li-ion battery wastes have been evaluated in batch and semi-batch leaching systems. In this preliminary study, HCl and H2O2 were used as leaching and reducing agents, respectively. The comparison of batch and semi-batch processes was carried out, obtaining an improvement from 40% to 70% in the metal mass extracted (i.e. Co and Li) for semi-batch experiments under the same experimental conditions. Effects of the initial concentration of reducing and leaching agents were evaluated for a semi-batch system in which the acid was continuously fed to maintain a constant pH value. From experimental results, it was found that the concentration of H2O2 plays an important role in the leaching process in terms of selectivity. For the experiments carried out using 0.1 M of HCl and 1 M of H2O2, the percentage of Li and Co extracted was 90% for a leaching time of 30 min. The double-controlled addition of HCl and H2O2 to the semi-batch system allows the reduction of the H2O2 concentration to 0.5 M. The optimization of reactants entails not only the decrease of their consumption but also maximize the selectivity of the reactions desired, which represents promising results for the environmental sustainability of the process. Further work will examine the fate of chloride ions in the process.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion batteries
Recycling
Semi-batch reactor
Leaching

Cerrillo-Gonzalez acknowledges the FPU2018/04295 grant obtained from the Spanish Ministry of Universities and the Postdoctoral grant (A.3.1) obtained from University of Malaga.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of semi-batch leaching reactor.

Volume evolution in a semi-batch reactor (V
0 = Initial solution volume; V
j = Volume before sampling; V
ja = Volume after sampling).

Effect of acid feeding operation on the leaching of (a) lithium and (b) cobalt; (c) pH profile and protons consumption and (d) mole ratio of Co/Li extracted in different operations described in Table 2: batch (B1) with 1.5 M HCl added initially (red triangle); batch (B2) with 1 M HCl added initially (black circles); semi-batch (S1) with 0.2 M HCl added initially and continuously to maintain pH at 0.88 (blue triangles) (other conditions: S/L ratio 50 g/L; 0.5 M H2O2; 25 °C). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Effect of initial H2O2 concentration of 0 M (S2, triangles), 0.25 M (S3, circles), 0.5 M (S1, diamonds) and 1 M (S4, squares) in a semi-batch reactor on leaching of lithium (a), cobalt (b) and consumption of H+ (c) as a function of time, and mole ratio of H+/Co2+ after 2 h (d) (other conditions: S/L ratio 50 g/L; 0.2 M HCl; 25 °C, total time 2 h).

Effect of initial HCl concentration of 0.2 M (S4, diamonds), 0.1 M (S5, circles), and 0.01 M (S6, triangles) in a semi-batch reactor on leaching of lithium (a), cobalt (b) and consumption of H+ (c) as a function of time over 1 h, and mole ratio of H+/Co2+ after 2 h (d) (other conditions: S/L ratio 50 g/L; 1 M H2O2; 25 °C).

Effect of double addition of HCl (0.1 M) and H2O2 (1, 0.5 or 0.25 M) in a semi-batch reactor on leaching of lithium (a), cobalt (b) and consumption of protons and H2O2 (c) as a function of time over 2 h and mole ratio of H+/Co2+ (d) after 2 h (other conditions: S/L ratio 50 g/L; pH 1.15, 25 °C, 2 h).
Reactant consumption and metal percentage extracted based on the reactor mode at 30 min.
Experimental conditions used in metal extraction experiments.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: (In all leaching experiments: S/L ratio = 50 g/ L, temperature = 25 °C, leaching time = 120 min)

head.ce:title: A comparison of batch and semi-batch reactors for leaching battery cathodes (LiCoO2) under controlled addition of HCl and H2O2

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, University of Malaga. Malaga, Spain
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, University of Malaga. Malaga, Spain

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recovery of cobalt and lithium from end-of-life Li-ion battery wastes have been evaluated in batch and semi-batch leaching systems. In this preliminary study, HCl and H2O2 were used as leaching and reducing agents, respectively. The comparison of batch and semi-batch processes was carried out, obtaining an improvement from 40% to 70% in the metal mass extracted (i.e. Co and Li) for semi-batch experiments under the same experimental conditions. Effects of the initial concentration of reducing and leaching agents were evaluated for a semi-batch system in which the acid was continuously fed to maintain a constant pH value. From experimental results, it was found that the concentration of H2O2 plays an important role in the leaching process in terms of selectivity. For the experiments carried out using 0.1 M of HCl and 1 M of H2O2, the percentage of Li and Co extracted was 90% for a leaching time of 30 min. The double-controlled addition of HCl and H2O2 to the semi-batch system allows the reduction of the H2O2 concentration to 0.5 M. The optimization of reactants entails not only the decrease of their consumption but also maximize the selectivity of the reactions desired, which represents promising results for the environmental sustainability of the process. Further work will examine the fate of chloride ions in the process.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrometallurgy involves the use of aqueous solutions to recover metals from ores. It has been widely used in mining and metallurgical industries. In the last decades, its use has been extended to the sector of lithium-ion battery (LIBs) recycling due to the characteristics of these wastes (Raj et al., 2022). The end-of-life LIBs contain valuable metals such as lithium, cobalt, nickel, and manganese, whose recovery is crucial for advancing the circular economy within the batteries industry (Mishra et al., 2022). Consequently, hydrometallurgical processes offer several appealing advantages, including high purity product, low energy consumption, and minimal gas emission, positioning them as one of the most promising methods for recycling of LIB waste (Yao et al., 2018).

The hydrometallurgical process generally includes two main stages: (i) leaching of the solid, and (ii) separation and recovery of the valuable compounds. Although the two steps are crucial, leaching is the key factor in the whole process since this step determines the technological complexity and the economic viability of the process (Lv et al., 2018). Leaching of battery cathodes is usually carried out using acids and reducing agents to convert metals contained in the solid into soluble metal ions. Although a wide range of lixiviants have been investigated to enhance the process, including mineral acids (H2SO4, HCl, HNO3) (Song et al., 2022) and organic acids (citric acid, tartaric acid, maleic acid) (Nayaka et al., 2019), only few acids, such as H2SO4 and HCl, remain currently viable in terms of industrial production (Baum et al., 2022). Nevertheless, high concentration of acids is normally required to achieve high yields of metals. This is an issue from process perspective due to the high consumption of reagents, not only during the leaching but also in the subsequent separation/recovery stages. The use of high acid concentrations results in a solution with low pH values even after the leaching, so a large amount of chemicals is needed to neutralize the solution in the recovery step which typically occurs at the pH range of 4–9 (Porvali et al., 2020b). Therefore, reducing the reactant consumption without decreasing the extraction yield is vital to improve the process economically and environmentally.

To optimize the hydrometallurgical process, it is important to understand the different reactions and mechanism that takes places during the leaching steps. In a previous work (Cerrillo-Gonzalez et al., 2020a), a mathematical model was developed to describe the mechanism of LiCoO2 dissolution in acid media. Basically, the leaching of LiCoO2 involves the reduction of Co3+ to soluble Co2+. In the absence of a stronger reducing agent, water oxidation transfers the electrons required to complete the reaction (Porvali et al., 2020a). In that case, the reaction takes place very slowly and Li+ and Co2+ are extracted from the LiCoO2 particles leaving a residue of Co3O4(s) around the LiCoO2 core (Eq. (1)). The formation of this insoluble crust reduces the leaching reaction rate due to the limited diffusion of the reactant from the aqueous solution to the surface of the unreacted core. This phenomenon emphasizes the use of reducing agent in the leaching process (Ferreira et al., 2009). Although several reducing agents, such as ascorbic acid (Li et al., 2012), hydroxylammonium chloride (Cerrillo-Gonzalez et al., 2024) and current collector scrap (Chernyaev et al., 2021; Joulié et al., 2017) have been investigated to be used in acid leaching of LIBs, H2O2 is still the most common reducing agent in research and the industrial scale (Baum et al., 2022). Its use as a reducing agent has the advantage of introducing no additional ions to the system; H2O2 is also well known as a strong oxidizing agent (H2O2/H2O; E
0 =1.776 V). However, cobalt in the LiCoO2 particles is in the stable form of Co(III) and requires reduction to Co(II) for its dissolution (Co3+/Co2+; E
0 =1.93 V). Due to the high reduction potential of Co(III)/Co(II) redox couple, H2O2 acts as a reducing agent against it, generally through oxygen evolution (O2/H2O2, E
0 =0.695 V). Its role in the leaching mechanism of LiCoO2 was also studied in an extension of the aforementioned model (Cerrillo-Gonzalez et al., 2022), in which it was assumed that the reactions in Eq. (1)–(4) occur simultaneously. The oxidation reactions of chloride ions by H2O2 or Co(III) have not been specifically studied in this work which would be interesting for further studies. The role of chloride ions in the redox reactions has been previously reported (Senanayake, 2011; Skudaev et al., 2011).

In terms of efficiency and productivity, the leaching system would be ideal in the case that only reaction (3) takes place (i.e. subproduct Co3O4 would not appear). However, the four reactions take place simultaneously during the leaching process. Therefore, the experimental conditions need to be optimized to promote the generation of the desired products. Conventionally, the leaching of LIBs is carried out in batch reactors where the solid is introduced together with the acid solution and reducing agent (Brückner et al., 2020). Then, the suspension is mixed until the leaching yield is reached. In this type of reactor, it is difficult to control the side-reactions that take place during the leaching process. However, the formation of undesired by-products can be decreased with the use of the reactor in a semi-batch mode (Worstell, 2015). For this configuration, one or more reactants are intermittently or continuously fed to the reactor. Controlling the reactant concentration can enhance the conversion and selectivity of the desired reactions, which results in a higher yield with a more effective use of the reactants.

Considering the reaction rates proposed in a previous study (Cerrillo-Gonzalez et al., 2022), for the reaction (1) and (3), the selectivity coefficient, S, could be defined with the following expression:
(5)
S =
r 3
r 1
=
k 3
k 1
·
n p
4 π
r c 2
V r
H core +
1 / 3
H 2
O 2
2
n p
4 π
r c 2
V r
H core +
=
k 3
k 1
·
H 2
O 2
2
H core +
2 / 3
where k1 and k3 are the reaction rate constant of reaction (1) and (3), respectively; r
c is the unreacted core radius (m); n
p is the number of particles (−); V
r is the reaction volume (m3), [H2O2] is the H2O2 concentration (mol m−3) and [H+ core] is the H+ion concentration (mol m−3). According to this expression, the selectivity coefficient increases with the H2O2 concentration while it decreases with the hydrogen ion concentration. Thus, selectivity will be maximized when the ratio between H2O2 and H+ concentration is high.

This work focuses on the application of a semi-batch reactor for the recovery of lithium and cobalt from spent LIBs. The initial concentration of leaching agent (HCl) and reducing agent (H2O2) were evaluated to maximize the process selectivity. To the best of our knowledge, there is no existing literature on the application of a semi-batch reactor for the evaluation of the leaching process of LIBs. This paper explores the utilization of a semi-batch reactor which allows to carry out leaching experiments under lower acid concentrations compared with batch reactors. In this work, a novel perspective of experimental system aims to contribute to a deeper understanding of the extraction process in a semi-continuous system.

Leaching experiments were carried out in a 0.25 L glass reactor equipped with a magnetic stirrer at 25 °C. The reactor was sealed with a four-necks ground-glass lids for containing a pH electrode, a HCl inlet, a H2O2 inlet and sampling. The experimental system is schematically illustrated in Fig. 1 . For all experiments, 100 mL of leaching solution was added to 5 g of LiCoO2 (composition in Table 1 ). The leaching solution was prepared using HCl (35% Panreac) and H2O2 (30% Panreac), which were selected to act as the leaching and reducing agent, respectively. Their initial concentrations for each experiment are detailed in Table 2 . During the experiments, except for test B1 and B2, the pH was measured (pH meter HACH sensION pH 3) and controlled continuously to ensure that the acid concentration was maintained constant in the reactor. For this, a 3 M HCl solution was continuously fed into the reactor to replenish the H+ ions consumed in the leaching process. The acid consumed by the reaction were determined based on the volume of acid added to the system to keep the pH at a constant value.

The experiments B1 and B2 (Batch), and S1 (Semi-batch) were focused on evaluating the effect of the controlling feed of the acid solution on the leaching reaction. The influence of H2O2 concentration on the LiCoO2 leaching reaction rates was examined in the experiments S1-S4. On the other hand, the effect of the initial HCl concentration was studied in the experiments S4-S6. Finally, the study of the double addition of reactants, both HCl and H2O2, into the reactor was performed in the experiments S7-S9. To determine the amount of H2O2 required by the reactions, it was assumed that the H2O2 was consumed following the stoichiometry of reaction (3): 1 mol H2O2/6 mol H+. Thus, the H2O2 was fed at a ratio based on the acid added to the system, using a 1 M H2O2 solution (i.e., 1 mL of 1 M H2O2 per 2 mL of 3 M HCl solution). It should be noted that the H2O2 solution was prepared and isolated in a dark container to avoid its photodegradation. All the experiments were carried out in duplicate to ensure their reproducibility.

Total leaching time was 2 h for all the experiments. Samples were collected from the reactor at 3, 6, 10, 15, 20, 30, 45, 60, 90 and 120 min. The volume of samples taken at each time varied depending on the volume of reagent (HCl and H2O2) added to the reactor to keep the volume of solution constant. Fig. 2 shows the theoretical changes in the volume during the experimental time; V
0 is the initial volume of the solution, V
j is the volume before sampling where j is the sample number, and V
ja is the volume after sampling. Therefore, the volume of the sample j at each time (V
j -V
ja ) is related to the volume of reactant added in each interval (V
j -V
(j-1)a ). It must be noted that the volume was kept constant throughout the experiment, with some negligible fluctuations due to the reactant addition and sampling.

All samples were filtered using a 0.6 μm glass-fiber (Macherey-Nagel GF-3) and analyzed via Atomic Absorption Spectrophotometry (Agilent 55AA) to quantify the amount of lithium and cobalt extracted. At the end of the experiments, the slurry was filtered with a vacuum filtration assembly (Boeco R-300) and the leach residue was dried at 70 °C for 24 h. Regarding the liquid phase, the filtrate was collected to quantify the final volume of the solution.

The percentage of metal extracted, both lithium and cobalt, was calculated according to the following expression:
(6)
x
t , i
% w / w
=
C
t , i
V j
m
0 , i
x 100
where x
t,i is the percentage of metal extracted (%w/w), C
t,i is the dissolved metal concentration (mg/ L), V
j is the solution volume at each time (L) calculated by considering the increase in volume due to the added HCl and H2O2 solutions and the subtraction of the volume of the samples withdrawn during the process, and m
0,i is the initial amount of metal in the solid sample (mg). The total content of cobalt and lithium in the solid samples was determined by microwave-assisted acid digestion (EPA method 3051 A).

Leaching of LiCoO2 was studied in batch (B1 and B2) and a semi-batch reactor (S1) to evaluate the effect of acid feeding method on the metal extraction. The three experiments were conducted at 25 °C and S/L ratio of 50 g/L using 0.5 M H2O2. The batch reactor in tests B1 and B2 was initially loaded with 100 mL of 1.5 M HCl solution and 1 M HCl solution, respectively. The semi-batch reactor was initially charged with 100 mL of 0.2 M HCl solution (S1), and the initial pH value of the solution (pH = 0.88) was kept constant by adding acid as required during the leaching reaction.

Fig. 3a-b show the transient percentage of lithium and cobalt extracted in the three experiments. It can be observed that the dissolution rate was faster in the semi-batch than in the batch mode, resulting in a higher percentage of metal extracted. Thus, after 60 min of the experiment, 84.3% of Li and 85.7% Co were extracted in the S1 test (semi-batch) while 70% of Li and 60% of Co were dissolved in the B1 test (1.5 M HCl batch). It must be noted that the protons used in both experiments were similar: 150 mmol in B1 and 140 mmol in S1 (the initial 20 mmol plus 120 mmol of H+ added to the system during the reaction time). The mole ratio Co/Li extracted is presented in Fig. 3d to evaluate the selectivity of reactions. As can be observed, this ratio was close to 1 in the S1 test, while it was between 0.8 and 0.9 in the B1 test. These results confirm that low acid concentration in the leaching medium can enhance the selectivity of the reaction (3). Based on these results, it could be concluded that high acid concentration enhances the leaching rate but decreases the selectivity of the desired reaction. This has been associated with the formation of a Co3O4 crust around the particles as concluded from previous works (Cerrillo-Gonzalez et al., 2020b). In batch reactors, where the initial acid concentration is higher than in the semi-continuous batch mode, the formation of the crust is promoted. Lower concentrations allow for a better control of particle dissolution preventing the formation of the Co3O4 crust.

In the B2 test (batch reactor at 1 M HCl), the selectivity was enhanced compared to the B1 test due to the initial acid concentration was lower. However, the conversion was lower in the B2 than in the B1 test. It is basically due to the depletion of protons in the system, as observed in the pH profile ( Fig. 3 c). After 30 min, the reactions were hindered in the test B2, resulting in a plateau of the Co and Li extracted. Therefore, it is not possible to enhance the selectivity and conversion at the same time in the batch reactor due to high acid concentration involves higher conversion but lower selectivity. In comparison, low acid concentration achieves higher selectivity but lower conversion. From these results, the use of semi-batch reactor enables the enhancement of both the selectivity and conversion of the leaching reactions. Therefore, the controlled addition of protons to the system not only increases the extraction yield but also optimizes the use of the reactants.

In this section, the influence of initial H2O2 and HCl concentration on the LiCoO2 dissolution was studied in a semi-batch reactor, in which the acid was continuously added to the system to keep the pH value of the solution constant.

The effect of initial H2O2 concentration on LiCoO2 leaching efficiency and selectivity was investigated varying H2O2 concentration from 0 to 1 M in a semi-batch reactor. Initially, the reactor was charged with the solid and a 0.2 M HCl solution with the selected H2O2 concentration. While the H2O2 was consumed during the reaction, the initial pH (pH = 0.88) was maintained constant by the controlled acid addition to the system based on its consumption. Experimental results are presented in Fig. 4 . The leaching efficiencies for lithium and cobalt are shown in Fig. 4a-b. The acid consumed in each experiment and the mole ratio of H+ consumed per Co2+ extracted are shown in Fig. 4c and Fig. 4d.

From the results in Fig. 4, it can be observed that LiCoO2 dissolution rate is highly dependent on the H2O2 concentration. In the absence of H2O2 (S2), only 27% Li+ and 14% Co2+ were extracted after 2 h, as a result of the leaching reactions (1) and (2). In this case, the mole ratio of H+/Co2+ was around 4, while Li+/Co2+ was close to 2:1, as expected from the stoichiometric ratio of reaction (1). It is known that the acidic leaching of lithium cobalt oxides is limited by the extremely strong chemical bond between cobalt and oxygen (Jiang et al., 2018).

The presence of H2O2 can effectively enhance the leaching process due to its capacity to reduce Co3+ to soluble Co2+ (He et al., 2017). It was found that an increase of H2O2 concentration not only results in an improvement of metal extracted, but also in an acceleration of the leaching reaction. For the experiments at 0.25 M H2O2 (in test S3), the percentage of Li and Co extracted was 58% and 56% after 2 h, respectively, while 93% of Li and Co was dissolved after 1 h when a 1 M H2O2 solution (in test S4) was used. Moreover, H2O2 concentration plays an important role in terms of the selectivity, as it was described in Eq. (5), in which a high H2O2 concentration maximizes the selectivity of the desired reaction. It agrees with the mol/mol ratio of H+ consumed/Co2+ extracted. In the case of 1 M H2O2 (in test S4), this ratio was close to 3, corresponding with the stoichiometric ratio of reaction (3). However, when the concentration of H2O2 was lower, this ratio increased, which would indicate that parallel reactions take places in the system. These results highlight the importance of optimizing the reducing agent concentration not only to maximizes the selectivity of the reactions but also to minimize the acid consumption which have a direct effect on the environmental and economical sustainability of the process.

Acid concentration is another parameter affecting not only the selectivity but also the leaching reaction rate. It was demonstrated previously that low acid concentration enhanced the extraction yield when a semi-batch reactor was used. However, if the acid concentration were too low, it could affect the reaction rate and even stop the reaction (Takacova et al., 2016). In this section, the influence of the initial HCl concentration on the LiCoO2 leaching was studied to optimize the extraction yield and leaching rate. Experiments were carried out in a semi-batch reactor where LiCoO2 (50 g/ L) and 1 M H2O2 were initially charged together with the initial amount of HCl, from 0.2 to 0.01 M (S4-S6). During experiments, the acid required was added intermittently to the system to keep the initial pH unchanged (target pH values were 0.88, 1.15 and 2.09 for experiments S4, S5 and S6, respectively).

According to the experimental results, shown in Fig. 5 (a, b) the decreases in HCl concentration from 0.2 M to 0.1 M did not have a significant effect as the the percentage of Li and Co extracted in both cases was around 90% after 30 min. Moreover, the leaching rate, as shown by the shape of the leaching curves, was almost identical for both acid concentrations during the reaction time. A reduction of the initial acid concentration by 10 times, to 0.01 M, resulted in a deceleration of the leaching rate, although the final percentage of Li and Co extracted was close to the percentage extracted with higher HCl concentrations. Once again, it was concluded that remaining low acid concentration enhances the selectivity of the desired reaction with H2O2 (Eq. (3)), as reflected in the mole ratio of H+/Co2+ ( Fig. 5 d) which was close to 3 in all cases.

From LIB recycling process point of view, leaching at low acid concentration by controlled acid addition is beneficial from an operational and economic perspective. Firstly, low acid concentration improves the selectivity resulting in higher percentage of cobalt extracted at shorter times. Furthermore, the low consumption of acid as well as the low remnant acid at the end of leaching benefits the subsequent metal separation stage, by precipitation (Yang et al., 2020). For example, the high initial acid concentration used in the traditional leaching systems, ranging from 2 to 4 M (Yao et al., 2018), to achieve appreciable leaching rates and yields, produce pregnant leach solutions which contain residual acid. Consequently, high amount of reactants would be needed to neutralize the leaching solution. To the best of our knowledge, although previous studies have evaluated the use of low acid concentration in leaching processes (Porvali et al., 2020b), the experimental conditions proposed in this work (i.e. solid to liquid ratio, acid concentration) has not been previously assessed.

In the previous sections, it was demonstrated that maintaining low acid and high H2O2 concentrations enhanced the selectivity and therefore, the extraction yield. However, the optimal concentration of H2O2 (1 M) was much higher than that was required to dissolve the solid sample according to the reaction stoichiometry. Previously, in the simple semi-batch reactor, only the acid was continuously fed to the reactor while H2O2 was loaded at the initial time. Thus, H2O2 concentration decreased during the reaction time. The double controlled addition of HCl and H2O2 was evaluated to optimize the initial H2O2 concentration. In the double semi-batch system, the required amount of H2O2 was added in accordance with the proton consumption and stoichiometry of the reaction (3) with the aim of maintaining a constant H2O2 concentration. Experiments were conducted by fixing the initial HCl concentration at 0.1 M (i.e., the pH was kept constant at 1.15) and varying the initial H2O2 concentration from 1 to 0.25 M (in tests S7-S9). Experimental results are shown in Fig. 6 .

According to the percentage of lithium and cobalt extracted (Fig. 6a-b), both the leaching rate and extraction yield were improved when the initial H2O2 concentration was increased from 0.25 to 1 M. The total solid dissolution was achieved in less than 30 min when an initial concentration of 1 M H2O2 (S7) was used, while the same results were obtained for 0.5 M H2O2 (S8) at 60 min. In the case of 0.25 M H2O2 (in test S9), 81% of Li and Co was dissolved after 2 h of experiment. However, the curve trend indicates that the complete dissolution of the solid could be achieved if longer leaching times had been applied. These results are in line with the fact that H2O2 concentration influences the leaching rate.

Fig. 7 shows a comparison, according to the reactor mode (batch, simple or double semi-batch), for the HCl and H2O2 consumed after 30 min of reaction and the percentage of Li and Co extracted. The semi-batch mode shows not only a significant improvement in the metal extracted, but also a more effective use of the reactants. For the same initial H2O2 concentration (0.5 M), the percentage of Li and Co extracted enhanced from 40% (B1) to 75% (S1) when the simple semi-batch mode was used, while the percentage increased to 86% (S8) in the double semi-batch mode. Moreover, it must be noted that semi-batch leaching not only increase the percentage of metal extracted but also decrease the consumption of acid. In test S1 and S8 (semi-batch mode), the total H+ consumed after 30 min was around 125 mmol, compared to 150 mmol in the batch reactor. The double semi-batch experiments (S8) allowed obtaining a similar percentage of metals extracted than in simple semi-batch mode (S5) for a leaching time of 30 min with the advantage of a decrease of reactant consumption (30% H2O2 and 13% of HCl).

According to experimental results, semi-batch mode has a high potential to be used in the leaching process of LIBs. Several advantages of this reactor configuration have been probed according to the results previously discussed. First, the semi-batch mode enables better control of solid dissolution, avoiding the surface structural reorganization, which improves the extraction yield. These results highlight the importance of studying the leaching kinetics and reaction mechanism and understanding the changes of solid surface structure during the process to optimize the operational conditions (Cerrillo-Gonzalez et al., 2022; Porvali et al., 2020b). The controlled addition of reactants optimized its consumption as can be seen from the ratio of mmol H+ consumed per mmol of Co2+ extracted in test B1 and S1 (7.30 and 3.2 for experiments B1 (batch) and S1 (semi-batch), respectively). Reducing the acid consumption also has advantages in the neutralization stage, which usually follows the leaching process, as described in Section 3.2.2.

The implementation of the proposal described in the current industrial hydrometallurgical process could be carried out without high additional investment costs because the reactor would be the same as in the batch system. Only the feeding of reactants would change with respect to the batch mode.

The optimization of the LiCoO2 leaching process in semi-batch mode involved evaluating key parameters within the process, specifically the initial concentrations of H2O2 and HCl as reducing and leaching agents. The findings revealed that maintaining low acid concentrations and high reducing agent concentrations (0.1 M HCl and 1 M H2O2) significantly enhances selectivity and improves the extraction yield of the leaching process (achieving 93% Li and 91% Co extraction). Additionally, the control addition of HCl and H2O2 (double semi-batch mode) was also evaluated to optimize the initial H2O2 concentration. The percentage of metals extracted, 99% Li and 96% Co, remained similar in the double semi-batch mode compared to the simple semi-batch mode, while reducing H2O2 consumption by 30%. These results demonstrate that the semi-batch mode not only maximizes reaction selectivity but also minimizes reactant consumption. Therefore, further exploration of semi-batch processes for extracting metals from LIBs should be carried out, especially for different LIB materials on an industrial scale.

Maria del Mar Cerrillo-Gonzalez: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Maria Villen-Guzman: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization. Alvaro Rivas-Bascon: Investigation, Formal analysis. Jose Miguel Rodriguez-Maroto: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Conceptualization. Juan Manuel Paz-Garcia: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors acknowledge the funding from the University of Malaga (B1-2021_35) and the TED2021-130756B-C31 grants funded by MCIN/AEI/10.13039/501100011033. Cerrillo-Gonzalez acknowledges the FPU2018/04295 grant obtained from the Spanish Ministry of Universities and the Postdoctoral grant (A.3.1) obtained from University of Malaga. Funding for open access charge: Universidad de Málaga/CBUA.
